examples.title: Algorithm: Hypervisor Resource Allocation
examples.description: Demonstrates how a Type 1 hypervisor allocates CPU and memory resources to multiple virtual machines using fair scheduling
examples.code:
ALGORITHM HypervisorResourceAllocation
INPUT: List of VMs requesting CPU time, Available CPU cycles per time slice
OUTPUT: CPU time allocation for each VM

BEGIN
 // Data structures
 CREATE vm_queue // Queue of active VMs
 CREATE vm_table // Table storing VM states (running, waiting, paused)
 CREATE resource_tracker // Tracks CPU/memory usage per VM
 
 PROCEDURE InitializeHypervisor(num_cores, total_memory_mb)
 SET available_cores = num_cores
 SET available_memory = total_memory_mb
 SET time_slice = 10 // milliseconds
 FOR each core DO
 SET core_state[core] = IDLE
 END FOR
 END PROCEDURE

 PROCEDURE AllocateResources(vm_id, vcpu_count, memory_mb)
 // Check if sufficient resources available
 IF vcpu_count > available_cores THEN
 RETURN ERROR "Insufficient CPU cores"
 END IF
 IF memory_mb > available_memory THEN
 RETURN ERROR "Insufficient memory"
 END IF
 
 // Reserve resources for VM
 SET available_cores = available_cores - vcpu_count
 SET available_memory = available_memory - memory_mb
 
 // Create VM entry
 CREATE vm_entry with id=vm_id, vcpus=vcpu_count, memory=memory_mb
 SET vm_entry.state = CREATED
 ADD vm_entry to vm_queue
 
 RETURN vm_entry
 END PROCEDURE

 PROCEDURE ScheduleVMs()
 // Fair round-robin scheduling among running VMs
 SET runnable_vms = []
 
 FOR each vm IN vm_queue DO
 IF vm.state == RUNNING THEN
 ADD vm to runnable_vms
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 // Distribute CPU time fairly
 SET time_per_vm = time_slice / SIZE(runnable_vms)
 
 FOR each vm IN runnable_vms DO
 EXECUTE vm for time_per_vm milliseconds
 IF vm.uses_quantum > 0 THEN
 // VM used its full time slice
 UPDATE vm.total_cpu_time = vm.total_cpu_time + time_per_vm
 ELSE
 // VM yielded early (I/O wait)
 UPDATE vm.total_cpu_time = vm.total_cpu_time + vm.uses_quantum
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 RETURN scheduling_complete
 END PROCEDURE

 PROCEDURE MigrateVM(vm_id, target_host)
 // Live migration: move VM from one host to another
 SET vm = FIND vm WHERE vm.id == vm_id
 SUSPEND vm // Pause VM execution
 TRANSFER vm.memory TO target_host
 TRANSFER vm.cpu_state TO target_host
 RESUME vm ON target_host
 REMOVE vm FROM vm_queue
 RETURN migration_complete
 END PROCEDURE

 // Main execution loop
 WHILE hypervisor_active DO
 WAIT FOR event // VM create, delete, I/O, timer
 
 IF event.type == VM_CREATE THEN
 CALL AllocateResources(event.vm_id, event.vcpus, event.memory)
 
 ELSE IF event.type == VM_SCHEDULE THEN
 CALL ScheduleVMs()
 
 ELSE IF event.type == VM_MIGRATE THEN
 CALL MigrateVM(event.vm_id, event.target_host)
 END IF
 END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates key hypervisor operations: resource allocation (checking available CPU cores and memory before creating a VM), fair CPU scheduling using round-robin algorithm, and live migration which suspends a VM, transfers its state, and resumes it on another host. The hypervisor acts as the VMM (Virtual Machine Monitor) layer between hardware and guest VMs.

examples.title: Python: VM Resource Manager Simulation
examples.description: Simulates a Type 2 hypervisor managing virtual machine resources including CPU, memory allocation, and VM lifecycle operations
examples.code:
"""
Virtual Machine Resource Manager Simulation
Demonstrates how a hypervisor manages VM resources
"""

class VirtualMachine:
 """Represents a single virtual machine"""
 
 def __init__(self, vm_id, name, vcpus, memory_mb, os_type):
 self.vm_id = vm_id
 self.name = name
 self.vcpus = vcpus # Number of virtual CPUs
 self.memory_mb = memory_mb # Memory in MB
 self.os_type = os_type # Guest OS type
 self.state = 'created' # created, running, paused, stopped
 self.cpu_time_used = 0 # Milliseconds of CPU time used
 self.snapshots = [] # List of snapshot states
 
 def __repr__(self):
 return f"VM({self.vm_id}: {self.name}, {self.state})"


class Hypervisor:
 """Type 2 Hypervisor simulation - manages multiple VMs"""
 
 def __init__(self, host_cores=8, host_memory_mb=32768):
 self.host_cores = host_cores
 self.host_memory_mb = host_memory_mb
 self.available_cores = host_cores
 self.available_memory = host_memory_mb
 self.vms = {} # Dictionary of VM ID -> VM object
 self.vm_counter = 0
 
 def create_vm(self, name, vcpus, memory_mb, os_type):
 """Create a new virtual machine with specified resources"""
 # Check resource availability
 if vcpus > self.available_cores:
 raise ValueError(f"Insufficient CPU cores: requested {vcpus}, available {self.available_cores}")
 if memory_mb > self.available_memory:
 raise ValueError(f"Insufficient memory: requested {memory_mb}MB, available {self.available_memory}MB")
 
 # Allocate resources
 self.vm_counter += 1
 vm_id = self.vm_counter
 vm = VirtualMachine(vm_id, name, vcpus, memory_mb, os_type)
 self.vms[vm_id] = vm
 
 # Reserve resources
 self.available_cores -= vcpus
 self.available_memory -= memory_mb
 
 print(f"[HYPERVISOR] Created VM {vm_id} ({name}) with {vcpus} vCPUs, {memory_mb}MB RAM")
 print(f" Resources remaining: {self.available_cores} cores, {self.available_memory}MB RAM")
 return vm
 
 def start_vm(self, vm_id):
 """Start a created VM"""
 if vm_id not in self.vms:
 raise ValueError(f"VM {vm_id} not found")
 
 vm = self.vms[vm_id]
 if vm.state != 'created' and vm.state != 'stopped':
 raise ValueError(f"Cannot start VM in state: {vm.state}")
 
 vm.state = 'running'
 print(f"[HYPERVISOR] Started VM {vm_id} ({vm.name})")
 return True
 
 def stop_vm(self, vm_id):
 """Stop a running VM and release its CPU resources"""
 if vm_id not in self.vms:
 raise ValueError(f"VM {vm_id} not found")
 
 vm = self.vms[vm_id]
 if vm.state != 'running':
 raise ValueError(f"VM {vm_id} is not running")
 
 vm.state = 'stopped'
 print(f"[HYPERVISOR] Stopped VM {vm_id} ({vm.name})")
 return True
 
 def delete_vm(self, vm_id):
 """Delete a VM and release all its resources"""
 if vm_id not in self.vms:
 raise ValueError(f"VM {vm_id} not found")
 
 vm = self.vms[vm_id]
 
 # Release resources back to host
 self.available_cores += vm.vcpus
 self.available_memory += vm.memory_mb
 
 del self.vms[vm_id]
 print(f"[HYPERVISOR] Deleted VM {vm_id}, resources freed")
 return True
 
 def create_snapshot(self, vm_id, snapshot_name):
 """Create a snapshot of VM's current state"""
 if vm_id not in self.vms:
 raise ValueError(f"VM {vm_id} not found")
 
 vm = self.vms[vm_id]
 snapshot = {
 'name': snapshot_name,
 'state': vm.state,
 'vcpus': vm.vcpus,
 'memory_mb': vm.memory_mb,
 'cpu_time': vm.cpu_time_used
 }
 vm.snapshots.append(snapshot)
 print(f"[HYPERVISOR] Created snapshot '{snapshot_name}' for VM {vm_id}")
 return snapshot
 
 def restore_snapshot(self, vm_id, snapshot_name):
 """Restore VM to a previous snapshot state"""
 if vm_id not in self.vms:
 raise ValueError(f"VM {vm_id} not found")
 
 vm = self.vms[vm_id]
 for snap in vm.snapshots:
 if snap['name'] == snapshot_name:
 vm.state = snap['state']
 vm.cpu_time_used = snap['cpu_time']
 print(f"[HYPERVISOR] Restored VM {vm_id} to snapshot '{snapshot_name}'")
 return True
 
 raise ValueError(f"Snapshot '{snapshot_name}' not found")
 
 def list_vms(self):
 """List all VMs and their current states"""
 print("\n" + "="*60)
 print(f"HOST RESOURCES: {self.available_cores}/{self.host_cores} cores, "
 f"{self.available_memory}/{self.host_memory_mb}MB RAM")
 print("-"*60)
 for vm in self.vms.values():
 print(f" VM {vm.vm_id}: {vm.name:20s} | OS: {vm.os_type:10s} | "
 f"State: {vm.state:8s} | vCPUs: {vm.vcpus} | RAM: {vm.memory_mb}MB")
 print("="*60 + "\n")


# Demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
 # Create a hypervisor with 8 cores and 32GB RAM
 hypervisor = Hypervisor(host_cores=8, host_memory_mb=32768)
 
 # Create multiple VMs (simulating server consolidation)
 try:
 web_server = hypervisor.create_vm("WebServer", vcpus=2, memory_mb=4096, os_type="Linux")
 db_server = hypervisor.create_vm("DatabaseServer", vcpus=4, memory_mb=8192, os_type="Linux")
 dev_env = hypervisor.create_vm("DevEnvironment", vcpus=1, memory_mb=2048, os_type="Windows")
 test_vm = hypervisor.create_vm("TestVM", vcpus=1, memory_mb=2048, os_type="Ubuntu")
 
 # Start all VMs
 hypervisor.start_vm(1)
 hypervisor.start_vm(2)
 hypervisor.start_vm(3)
 hypervisor.start_vm(4)
 
 # List all VMs
 hypervisor.list_vms()
 
 # Create a snapshot for backup
 hypervisor.create_snapshot(1, "pre-update-backup")
 
 # Simulate stopping test VM to free resources
 hypervisor.stop_vm(4)
 hypervisor.delete_vm(4)
 
 # Show updated state
 hypervisor.list_vms()
 
 except ValueError as e:
 print(f"Error: {e}")
examples.explanation: This Python simulation demonstrates key hypervisor concepts: resource allocation when creating VMs (checking available CPU cores and memory), VM lifecycle management (create, start, stop, delete), snapshot creation and restoration for backup/recovery, and resource tracking. It shows how a Type 2 hypervisor manages multiple guest VMs on a single host system, similar to how VirtualBox or VMware Workstation operates.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for VM operations where n is number of VMs
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of VMs stored

examples.title: C: Virtual Memory and Address Space Management
examples.description: Demonstrates low-level memory management concepts that hypervisors use, including virtual address translation and page table management
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates the low-level memory virtualization concepts that hypervisors use: page table management (structs storing valid bits, frame numbers, permissions), demand paging (allocating physical frames on demand), virtual-to-physical address translation (simulating MMU functionality), and memory isolation (showing how different VMs can use the same virtual addresses but map to different physical frames). This mirrors how VMware, KVM, and other hypervisors provide each VM with its own isolated virtual address space.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for address translation, O(n) for frame allocation where n is total frames
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is physical memory frames + O(m) per VM for page tables

examples.title: Java: JVM Bytecode and Class Loading Demonstration
examples.description: Demonstrates how Java bytecode is executed by the JVM, showing the compilation process and class loading mechanism
examples.explanation: This Java program demonstrates JVM concepts: bytecode execution (how Java source compiles to .class files and runs on JVM), stack-based operations (JVM uses operand stack for calculations), method invocation (invokevirtual, invokestatic bytecode instructions), object creation on heap (new instruction), array operations (newarray, aaload, aastore), JIT compilation (showing how hot methods are compiled for performance), class loading hierarchy (bootstrap, extension, application class loaders), and memory management (heap, garbage collection). The JVM is an application-level virtual machine that provides platform independence by abstracting the underlying hardware.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for loop operations, O(1) for memory queries
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for object allocations in demo
